serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of vegetarian beehoon with mock meat is approximately 250 g, typical of a hawker centre or food court serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Beehoon with Mock Meat — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetarian Beehoon with Mock Meat (cr_id F06010001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — Glycemic Index entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for vegetarian beehoon with mock meat. GI of 58 is estimated from rice vermicelli (boiled) as the dominant carbohydrate component, per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Stir-frying with oil, protein from mock meat, and dietary fibre (2.3 g/100g) are expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain boiled vermicelli. Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 58 × 57.25 / 100 ≈ 33.2 (based on 250 g serving providing 57.25 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1063 mg sodium, representing 47% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per serving and request reduced soy sauce to keep sodium intake below 650 mg per meal; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli may partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a refined carbohydrate with a medium GI (~58); a 250 g serving provides approximately 57 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~33, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to approximately 150 g (providing ~34 g carbohydrate and GL ~20), choose extra non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the refined rice noodle base can cause moderate glycaemic excursions despite the protein and fibre content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 425 mg/100 g is elevated, and total fat at 4.6 g/100 g is moderate; saturated fat is estimated at 0.7 g/100 g, which is low, and cholesterol is negligible as the dish is plant-based.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content (425 mg/100 g) and limit portion to 150 g; the plant-based mock meat contributes soy protein which may have a modest LDL-lowering effect, but the overall sodium load remains the primary cardiovascular concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Beehoon with Mock Meat is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of thin rice vermicelli noodles (beehoon) stir-fried with beansprouts, green onions (spring onions), and soy-based mock meat, seasoned with soy sauce and cooked in vegetable oil. The dish is fully plant-based and is commonly found at vegetarian stalls in hawker centres and food courts across Singapore. The texture is light and slightly chewy from the rice noodles, with a savoury umami flavour derived from the soy sauce and mock meat. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 152 kcal, 4.4 g protein, 4.6 g fat, and 22.9 g carbohydrate, with 2.3 g dietary fibre and 425 mg sodium. The high sodium content (425 mg/100 g) is the primary clinical concern, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without portion control and sauce reduction. For individuals with type 2 diabetes, the moderate glycaemic index (~58, medium) and moderate carbohydrate content require portion management. The dish is suitable for vegetarians and vegans, is dairy-free, and is generally considered halal. It is a common working lunch option in Singapore and is culturally associated with Buddhist vegetarian cuisine traditions.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06010001) at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (rice vermicelli, boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Saturated fat and sugar are estimated from component analysis consistent with HPB data patterns for similar dishes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific vegetarian beehoon with mock meat preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06010001) as of 2026. 2. saturated_fat_g (0.7 g/100g) and sugar_g (1.5 g/100g) are estimated from component analysis of similar HPB-listed stir-fried rice noodle dishes; these values are plausible but not directly reported in the source entry. 3. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish is fully plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. FoodEx2 code A0FJP and FoodOn URI FOODON_00002473 are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for vegetarian beehoon with mock meat.
